goal: Let security admins launch adversarial red team campaigns via SMS and WhatsApp (v1), targeting individual users, groups, or both, with architecture designed to expand to Viber, Telegram, and vishing.
user_segments: security leadership / CISO (approver, if dual-auth required)
success_metrics: Not defined in brief — must be established before design begins
constraints: Architecture must support Viber, Telegram, and vishing from day one
Launch flow lives inside or alongside existing campaign scheduler
Base UI pattern: SMS phishing wizard — inherit what applies, flag what changes
RBAC: Admin-initiated; sub-role read-only visibility TBD
WhatsApp Business API requires template pre-approval for bulk sends — may limit arbitrary custom lures
Individual user targeting is a net-new targeting model not present in simulations
assumptions: Red team campaigns are adversarial exercises distinct from simulations in purpose and compliance stakes
SMS phishing wizard patterns are directly reusable for the wizard skeleton and component patterns
WhatsApp delivery is feasible under WhatsApp Business API — not yet validated by Eng
Vishing architecture (Dune-managed VOIP vs. manual outcome recording) is undecided
Compliance model for red team campaigns is at least as strict as simulations — may be stricter
gaps: Separate campaign type vs. extension of existing types — foundational decision not made
Risk score pipeline: isolated vs. integrated with simulation pipeline — not decided
Compliance/consent requirements for red team campaigns not defined
WhatsApp Business API feasibility for arbitrary custom lures not validated by Eng
Multi-channel targeting model not selected (campaign-level, group-level, or user-level)
Individual user targeting scope and RBAC requirements not defined
Delivery failure fallback behavior (WhatsApp fails → SMS, or error, or silent exclude) not decided
Remediation automation suppression model for adversarial exercises not addressed
RBAC sub-role visibility into red team results not confirmed
Template library model not confirmed (shared with simulations or red-team-specific)
edge_cases: WhatsApp delivery failure for a target: fallback, error, or silent exclude — undefined
Target appears in both individual list and a targeted group — duplicate contact risk
Zero targets with WhatsApp accounts in selected audience — 0% channel coverage equivalent
WhatsApp message template pending API approval at launch time
Admin cancels mid-execution with mixed delivery state (some SMS sent, some WhatsApp sent)
Target has opted out of SMS — WhatsApp opt-out is platform-level, not Dune-level
Remediation auto-fires on red team click — alerts target before debrief, breaks adversarial exercise
WhatsApp link preview exposes debrief page title/URL before target taps link
Vishing call not answered — no outcome recorded unless admin manually logs it
Debrief page rendered in WhatsApp in-app browser — different rendering constraints than native mobile browser
Multi-channel campaign results table: per-channel columns may exceed readable width on 1024px breakpoint

critical_questions.question: Is red team a separate campaign type with its own nav, data model, and reporting — or an extension of existing simulation types with additional channel and targeting options?
Do red team campaign results feed the same risk score pipeline as simulations, or are they isolated to a separate red team reporting surface?
Is the existing SMS phishing compliance acknowledgment (customer confirms lawful basis) sufficient for red team, or do red team campaigns require a higher-level approval step?
Can Dune send arbitrary custom red team messages via WhatsApp Business API, or are bulk sends restricted to pre-registered templates? Does this limit v1 WhatsApp scope?
When WhatsApp delivery fails: fall back to SMS, surface per-user error, or silently exclude? This decision drives the multi-channel delivery status model.
Should red team campaigns suppress remediation automation (training assignment, manager notification) to avoid alerting the target that they were tested?
What does 'individual user' targeting mean exactly — single named user, ad-hoc list, or any non-group subset? Does individual targeting require a second admin to approve?
If a user appears in both the individual list and a targeted group in the same campaign, are they contacted once or twice? Who resolves the deduplication?
Do sub-admin roles get read-only visibility into red team campaign results, or are results restricted to the launching admin and security leadership?

What is the vishing architecture — Dune-managed outbound VOIP with auto-recorded outcomes, or manual operator calls with Dune as an outcome-recording tool? These produce completely different design surfaces.

design_risks: WhatsApp Business API bulk-send constraints (template pre-approval, opt-in) may prevent arbitrary custom lures — must validate before any WhatsApp UI is designed
Remediation auto-trigger alerts target that they were tested, breaking the adversarial exercise — suppression must be a first-class option in the wizard's remediation step
Multi-channel targeting model (campaign-level, group-level, user-level) produces three different interaction patterns — cannot begin wizard design without a decision
Individual + group overlap creates duplicate contact risk — audience step must surface and resolve overlaps explicitly
Compliance acknowledgment at campaign level may be insufficient for red team — customers may launch without appropriate internal authorization if the bar is too low
Navigation ambiguity (inside or alongside campaign scheduler) risks mixing red team and simulation campaigns in the same list, creating confusion about purpose and results
teaching_notes: Inherit from sms-phishing: wizard skeleton (7-step, Stillsuit DS v2), audience selector, coverage indicator, compliance acknowledgment, test send step, delivery spread, campaign list + detail view layout

Change vs. sms-phishing: channel selector (new pattern), multi-dimensional coverage indicator (per channel), remediation suppression option, campaign type badge (Red Team vs. Simulation), risk pipeline routing, template library model
WhatsApp opens links in in-app browser — rendering differs from native mobile browser; link previews before tap may expose debrief page meta data
Vishing requires architecture clarification before design: Dune-managed VOIP vs. manual outcome recording are different surfaces
Run /competitor-intelligence on KnowBe4 and Proofpoint before designing multi-channel targeting — no Dune precedent exists
Red team campaigns are adversarial assessments, not educational exercises — this distinction drives compliance, RBAC, remediation, and risk pipeline design
